data.title: SMH: AI Mania In Full Swing - Peaking Volumes Imply Potential Implosion (Downgrade)
data.description:
SMH's Rally Overly Done, As The Market Enters An AI Bubble

I previously covered the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH) in June 2025, discussing how the semiconductor sector had reported a massive reversal after the worst of the market-wide meltdown in April 2025 and the market's overblown fears surrounding the potentially slowing AI spending cadence.

With the ETF's top three holdings delivering double-beat performances and raised future guidance, I had believed that the multi-year cloud spending trends remained robust, aided by the ongoing de-escalation in the trade war between the US/China, resulting in my reiterated Buy rating then.

SMH 1Y ETF Price

Since then, the bulls have continued to defend SMH's upward momentum by +29.6% compared to the wider market at +11.8%, thanks to the multi-year AI super cycle as reiterated by numerous hyperscalers in the Q2'25 earnings season and the weeks after that.

For reference, five of the world's largest hyperscalers by market share, Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG), Alibaba (BABA), and Oracle (ORCL) in respective order, have been reporting robust cloud-related revenues while hinting at higher capex trends in 2026.

Many other players, including numerous neoclouds and start-ups, have also highlighted the voracious appetite for computing power while reporting growing deals/accretive acquisitions/increased fundings, with the immense rally also underscoring why "OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has reportedly said that he believes AI could be in a bubble."

The two AI market leaders, Nvidia (NVDA) and OpenAI, have further fed the FOMO rally, with the GPU giant committing a $100B investment in the AI SaaS company contingent on the deployment of at least 10 GW of Nvidia systems, and H1'26 expected to commence the first rollout of the Vera Rubin platform.

The same has been observed in Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), with the Lisa Su-led company issuing OpenAI a warrant of approximately 160M shares, or the equivalent of 10% of the former's equity, and the latter expected to deploy 6 GW of AMD's systems moving forward.

This is on top of OpenAI supposedly committing to a >$10B of orders with Broadcom (AVGO), with it naturally buoying the market's conviction surrounding the growing demand for custom AI accelerator chips while lending further credence to the IDTechEx's projection of the overall AI chips demand growing at an accelerated CAGR of +14% to $453B by 2030.

These developments underscore the highly durable (and extremely hefty) AI-related spending trends for the next few years, with it naturally exemplifying the robust prospects of these semiconductor companies along with the world's global foundry, namely, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM).

My optimism for the latter, arises from the fact that TSM commands a growing global foundry share at 70.2% in Q2'25 (+2.6 points QoQ/+7.9 YoY) and the foundry 2.0 share at 35% in Q1'25 (inline QoQ/+0.9 points YoY), with the undisputed market leader set to enjoy robust top/bottom-line performance as the appetite for AI chips proves insatiable.

This is significantly aided by the foundry's ongoing manufacturing expansion globally across Taiwan, Arizona in the US, Japan, and Germany, allowing them to increasingly grow its production capacity, top/bottom-line performance, and consequently, market share moving forward.

These reasons are also why SMH has been able to enjoy a rich +73.1% price gain since the April 2025 bottom, since NVDA, TSM, and AVGO, being the three largest holdings comprising 36.24% of the ETF, have reported immense rallies by +79.3%, +107.9%, and +108% over the same time period, respectively.

Thanks to the growing AI chip demand and the higher foundry capacity utilization, ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) (OTCPK:ASMLF), the world's most valuable lithography company, is likely to consistently report higher net lithography sales and net service/field option sales as well, with it underscoring its highly strategic razor and blade business model.

This is especially since experts have highlighted how data center GPUs may only "survive for 1 to 2 years, or up to 3 years," with the "significant stress" on chips potentially triggering an intensified replacement cycle and, subsequently, accelerated lithography upgrades/servicing cadence.

Another of SMH's holdings, Micron Technology (MU), has proven itself as a leader in the memory market as well, thanks to its close collaboration with NVDA and the growing hyperscaler wins with six market leaders, which has contributed to the growing HBM3E/HBM4 market share and the richer top/bottom lines.

Combined with the end of the prior memory inventory correction in FY2023 and the potentially sold-out position through the end of 2026, it is apparent that MU has been able to capitalize on the ongoing AI boom.

This is significantly aided by the ongoing recovery in the automotive and industrial end markets, with the ADAS/AI-enhanced capabilities already triggering an increased adoption of higher-performance memory solutions and the improved pricing mix contributing to the recovering profit margins nearer to pre-pandemic levels.

A similar cadence has been reported by QUALCOMM (NASDAQ:QCOM) (NEOE:QCOM:CA) as well, as observed in the extremely rich auto/IoT YTD revenues growth profile of +34% YoY and the company seemingly on track to achieving the auto/IoT revenues of $22B in FY2029, expanding at a CAGR of +21.4% from FY2024 levels of $8.33B.

Combined with QCOM's expanding AI use cases on the smartphone platform, along with the diversification to the CPU/smart wearables end markets, it goes without saying that the wider semiconductor market has been well buoyed by the ongoing AI boom and the recovering industrial/auto/IoT end markets.

So, Is SMH ETF A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SMH 5Y ETF Price

These reasons may also be why SMH has hit new peaks of $348s by the time of writing while running away from the 50/100/200-day averages, with it apparent that the AI mania has reached fever pitch levels as FOMO traders/investors also feed the ongoing rally nearing the Q3'25 earnings season.

SMH Valuations

As a result of the immense rally, it is unsurprising that SMH, at a P/E of 43.67x, along with other semiconductor-focused ETFs, appears to be on the expensive side, compared to the wider market at 34.84x.

This is especially since the ETF's top three holdings:

  • NVDA at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.21x compared to the 5Y mean of 47.78x,
  • TSM at 30.69x/22.54x,
  • AVGO at 49.79x/23.41x, respectively,

They are rather inflated while offering interested investors a reduced margin of safety.

SMH's Overbought Position

SMH's technical indicators also suggest its overbought position, as observed in the peaking Relative Strength Index (RSI) and the trading volumes hitting historical peaks, with it remaining to be seen how long the upward momentum may last, depending on the holdings' results in the upcoming earnings calls.

While I have personally invested in many of the ETF's holdings and in the ETF itself, I believe that the investors' buying power are likely to be exhausted soon, and a pullback is rather likely, with it presenting risks to its near-term investment thesis.

As a result, I prefer to prudently downgrade SMH to a Hold here while urging interested investors to accumulate capital and wait for a deep retracement to occur before adding afterwards for an improved margin of safety.

Pending that speculative event, let's wait on the sidelines for now.

data.title: SoundHound AI: Once-In-A-Generation Tech Platform Still In Its Infancy
data.description:
Sometimes, the idea of a certain technological paradigm shift is so inevitable that resisting it seems almost futile. When tech solutions offer utility that drives undeniable cost efficiencies, entire industries are forced to adapt. One such megatrend that's very clearly playing out is the agentic AI transformation, and SoundHound AI (NASDAQ:SOUN) seems to be right at the center of it.

This is my fourth coverage of SoundHound AI, and I'm pretty happy with how my predictions of the stock's performance have turned out so far:

As can be seen above, I have called for strong buy and buy positions on the stock at $10, $13, and $14. Today, SoundHound AI trades at a price of ~$19, and, as I will argue in this assessment, I still feel that the value proposition is intact and arguably even stronger than before.

The company recently announced the deployment of its Amelia 7 agentic AI platform with Apivia Courtage, a major European insurer, marking another major step in its expansion into enterprise AI. The move demonstrates the real-world impact of SoundHound's tech in improving productivity and customer experience in diverse industries.

In this article, I break down why this development validates SoundHound’s long-term strategy and, more importantly, reinforces the case for continued shareholder value creation.

As such, I'm maintaining my buy stance on SoundHound AI.

SoundHound Expands Agentic AI Partnership with Apivia Courtage

Yesterday, SoundHound AI announced a collaborative partnership with the French wholesale insurance broker, Apivia Courtage. Apivia Courtage is a subsidiary of the AEMA Group, which is also among the world’s largest mutual insurance organizations.

As per the announcement, Apivia Courtage will be deploying SoundHound AI’s Amelia 7 agentic AI platform throughout its various contact centers. As such, the Amelia platform will be in charge of handling a wide range of customer interactions, which include identity verification, answering contract-related questions, updating personal information, calculating financial impacts on insurance policies, and scheduling meetings with human advisors.

This collaboration actually builds on an older partnership between the two companies, going back to 2023, which saw Apivia report a 20% boost in its contact center productivity with the help of earlier SoundHound tool deployments. The newly announced deployment extends earlier agentic capabilities by introducing fully agentic AI agents, which are capable of reasoning, planning, and executing complex multi-intent tasks without human escalation.

The newly deployed platform will operate across multiple channels, such as voice, chat, and text, each of which leverages SoundHound's advanced speech recognition and natural language understanding to ensure seamless and more "human" interactions. This setup allows for a lower labor burden while allowing human teams to focus more on higher-value tasks (such as relationship management, etc.).

Strategic Implications for SoundHound

While on the surface there's a lot to appreciate about the advantages the collaboration brings to both parties, I think there's quite clearly a deeper and more strategic narrative about SoundHound AI that's being shaped and is easy to overlook. This relates to the company's leadership positioning in agentic AI as a foundational enterprise platform with a near dominance of the voice AI dimension.

Considering that Amelia 7 is seeing an expanded deployment in an industry as highly regulated and compliance-sensitive as insurance, I believe it seems very clear that SoundHound AI is actually very capable of owning the layer of AI that enterprises of multiple industries can potentially turn to for mission-critical, multi-channel, agentic interactions. This is especially true in cases where there is a need to reason, act, and integrate across human workflows at scale.

With this arrangement, SoundHound AI has successfully demonstrated enterprise trust as well as compliance capability, which will surely prove as a future proof point for other regulated and high-value industries, such as banking, healthcare, and energy. Similarly, the company has also managed to visibly showcase impressive ROI, where organizations report double-digit productivity boosts, making such an investment necessary from a competitive sense. The benefits would directly flow into company margins by significantly driving down operational costs per customer engagement (while minimizing error rates).

What I find most promising, however, is that every SoundHound AI deployment actually reinforces the company's platform moat through network effects. What this means is each implementation generates proprietary interaction data and hones its agentic reasoning capabilities to a higher standard than before.

Artificial intelligence, by its very nature, improves the quality of its output with exposure to real-world data and complex scenarios. Every deployment allows SoundHound AI to capture more nuances in language, better decipher customer intents, and gain a technical edge in industry-specific workflows that cannot be fully replicated in a lab. This proprietary data then pretty much feeds back into the platform, which enhances accuracy, reasoning, and contextual understanding of Amelia 7 for future clients in related or even in different industries.

In essence, through these wins, SoundHound AI is actually widening the gap between itself as the first meaningful mover in the space and potentially new entrants that may look to disrupt. It's doing so by accumulating proprietary data, refining its agentic reasoning, and deepening industry-specific expertise. This is a compounding advantage that makes SOUN stock the perfect long-term growth vehicle that many are seeking out in the tech and in the AI space.

Valuation Assessment

At its current price of almost $19, SoundHound AI holds a market capitalization of $7.4 billion. To some, this might seem like a stretch, considering that the company reports a trailing twelve-month revenue figure of only $131 million, without even turning in a profit at its current stage. This puts the stock's PS ratio at ~57, which is up from the 45x from my coverage in August.

When a stock is trading at 57x its revenue, the market is clearly expecting the company to deliver value that's a whole order of magnitude higher than it's currently reeling in. In SoundHound's case, I see this as being easily justifiable, as the company seems quite able to successfully scale its agentic AI platform across multiple large enterprise clients and replicate the productivity gains seen at Apivia Courtage. This playbook makes the company very valuable, allowing it to unlock massive TAM in high-value industries like banking, healthcare, and telecommunications, which are in addition to the automotive and service industries it's already focused upon.

I believe that investors should not be distracted by the $131 million revenue figure and instead consider that its total backlog amounts to about $1.2 billion (which makes its current $7.4 billion market cap more palatable). In fact, what I'm most compelled by is the actual scale of the TAM the company is targeting and how quickly it could realistically capture it. The management estimates the size of this opportunity to be at around $140 billion, at the very least. For context, SoundHound's revenue currently touches <0.1% of that estimation. Capturing 1% of this market would see the company's revenue increase 10x. I feel that the company is capable of capturing a meaningful chunk (40%-plus) of this market in the upcoming years, given its clear technical edge, its proven track record of enterprise deployments across different industries, and the meaningful ROI it delivers to clients.

I see SOUN as a phenomenal growth stock that's likely to compound through the next few years into the most dominant player in the enterprise agentic AI space.

As such, I reiterate my buy rating on the stock.

Risk to Thesis

While the agentic arena is not exactly crowded right now, from a competitive sense, I feel that big tech will eventually notice the opportunity in voice AI and AI-driven customer engagement at scale. Right now, while it may seem that SoundHound is going to own this layer of the AI stack, the largest tech companies out there, whether Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), or even Apple (AAPL), could possibly pursue a path that involves bundling voice AI and agentic capabilities into products and platforms. Unlike SoundHound, these players have massive distribution channels that would enable them to reach hundreds of millions of users across the world instantly.

This is a risk that investors need to keep in mind because it could cut short the company's lofty ambitions, which would bring the stock's high multiples back down to earth.

This being said, however, I would not be too paranoid about this risk because SoundHound AI does hold the advantage of specialized expertise and deep integrations. The company has institutional knowledge built up over 20 years, which cannot exactly be replicated overnight.

Investor Takeaway

SoundHound AI is a perfect example of a company positioned at the heart of an inevitable technological inflection. Its strategy of embedding its platforms within enterprise ecosystems ensures both resilience and compounding learning effects. Moving forward, as the agentic layer becomes a necessity rather than an option, I strongly believe that SoundHound’s AI platform stands to benefit from the network effects of adoption and data.

data.title: S&P Global: AI Analyzes, But Humans Decide (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
AI Enthusiasm Does Not Apply To Every Company

I often hear talk about a potential AI bubble, but even amid this enthusiasm, you can still find solid, undervalued businesses. On one hand, AI is beneficial for all those companies that invest heavily in cloud computing/automation, but on the other hand, it questions all those companies with a traditional business model.

In particular, market data firms are going through a complex period since FactSet downwardly revised its revenue guidance for the fiscal year. As I explained in a previous article, I think the market reaction was totally irrational; after all, subscriptions and revenue are still experiencing a positive growth rate. Of course, lower growth than anticipated, but I don’t think this justifies a drop comparable to what happened in the middle of the Great Financial Crisis.

Where does all this pessimism come from?

The market narrative is that AI will gradually replace market data firms, something that I believe is very unlikely. If anything, AI can enhance their platforms, not replace them.

In this general panic that began with FactSet (FDS), even exceptional companies such as S&P Global Inc. (NYSE:SPGI) are being dragged down, falling about 15% from its all-time high.

As you can see from this chart, such a drop doesn’t happen very often, and every time it has occurred, the stock usually recovered pretty fast: a great company can’t stay cheap for long.

When the market eventually recognizes that AI can’t replace this financial giant, but it will be a tool to enhance it, then it will probably be too late to buy SPGI.

Why AI Can’t Replace SPGI

At present, I believe there is excessive enthusiasm surrounding AI, as if it were going to wipe out millions and millions of jobs in the coming years. I don't want to downplay recent technological advances; I just don't believe that human judgment no longer carries any weight.

Let’s take, for example, the SPGI rating segment, responsible for 30% of revenue and 47% of operating income.

AI has been a hot topic for years now, yet I see a constant revenue growth and a very high profitability (large improvements over the last decade). AI should already have replaced part of this business, but in my opinion, this is not happening for two reasons.

The first one is that we are still far from fully trusting AI with such an important issue. Credit rating needs human judgment, years of experience, and developed interpretation skills. AI-based rating would rely just on objective components, without involving any critical thinking ability. Here is an example.

Let’s assume that we need to assess a rating for a company that has been struggling for the past three years, but it has just announced a new CEO. Since the latter is not quantitative data, it is complicated for an AI model to figure out how important this information is. Therefore, the AI-based rating might not properly weigh a relevant component.

This is just an example, but I think you got the point. AI struggles to elaborate on qualitative data; that’s why human judgment is still the best choice.

The second reason is just a matter of reputation. S&P Global, alongside Fitch and Moody’s, has been dominating the rating industry for decades, and has accumulated a tremendous amount of experience in everything related to it. It has thousands and thousands of trusted clients, and it has succeeded in overcoming every recession and regulatory issue. To become a credit rating company, it is required to be identified as a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization, a long and complex process to deal with.

A new AI model cannot even be compared to any of these giants, simply because no one would rely just on a machine for their investment decisions. We are humans, and we only trust other humans when it comes to making important decisions.

Rather than replacing market data companies, I think AI can potentially be a growth driver. Automating operating tasks, speeding up data interpretation, are all pros to further improve the profitability of this industry (already quite high).

Let’s now take a look at the Indices business segment, responsible for 12% of total revenue and 20% of total operating income.

Here as well, despite the AI hype going on for years, there is no sign of growth slowing down. The reason is pretty simple: the human judgment wins once again.

S&P Global indices are used worldwide as a benchmark by most analysts; the S&P 500 index is the most important one to evaluate stock market performance. After decades of dominance in this industry, it is unlikely, to say the least, that AI-based indices will replace those we are all familiar with.

In this industry, there is no necessity for this kind of innovation, which is why I am rather confident that SPGI indexes will keep providing increasing revenue over the long term.

Finally, let's look at the Market Intelligence segment, responsible for 32% of total revenue and 16% of total operating income.

Compared to previous segments, this one doesn’t present the same profitability, but it remains AI replacement-proof.

The Market Intelligence segment includes multiple businesses, from firm consulting to offering solutions for asset managers/investment bankers, as well as providing an investing desktop platform called S&P Capital IQ. Overall, it is a wide-ranging segment and makes critical reasoning its strong point.

Over the decades, it has established a bond of trust between S&P Global and its clients, which is why I don’t think the latter will prefer a non-empathetic smart chatbot to consultants that have been helping them for years.

In this case as well, I don’t see how AI can be considered an imminent problem for this business segment. At best, it can become a useful tool at the consultant's disposal. After all, it's a bit like the medical field: if you're sick, do you trust AI's opinion more than your doctor's?

Conclusion

I analyzed the segments responsible for 83% of SPGI’s operating income, and frankly, I am not worried at all about the current market narrative. I don’t think this is the end of market data firms; on the contrary, AI might be a further growth catalyst.

This might be the right time to add some shares of SPGI, or more in general, some shares of these kinds of companies.

According to the Price/Free Cash Flow multiple, its current valuation seems quite cheap compared to the historical average, which is why I think a buy rating is deserved. So this means an upward rating compared to my last article on SPGI. Back then, the stock was traded at $535, so it was worth the wait.

I can’t know in advance if this is the bottom, but I genuinely don’t care: the more it drops, the more I buy. This is what I always do when it comes to investing in great businesses. In my opinion, SPGI has the potential to be a permanent company in my portfolio, so a buy-and-hold strategy is definitely the most appropriate.

However, I didn’t buy any shares yet because of what is happening to FactSet right now. Basically, I've invested so much in the latter that I can't do anything else before the end of the month.

The stock is priced as if we were in the middle of a financial crisis, but the GDP is growing more than 3%, and FactSet’s revenue and users are both growing. If you want to know more about it, I suggest you read this article.

data.title: The Market Loves Rollins. Maybe Too Much.
data.description:
Rollins, Inc. (NYSE:ROL) is one of the best businesses we can find in this market. It is a stable, boring business that is growing at a high pace and showing little fluctuations. However, in the past, I always considered the stock to be a little too expensive for being a good investment and in my last article, which was published at the beginning of this year, I rated the stock a “Hold” once again. In the conclusion of my last article I wrote:

In the end, we should still be very cautious about an investment in Rollins. We could make the case for Rollins being fairly valued, but always have to keep in mind that high-growth rates are necessary. Rollins is certainly not a bargain and although the business is recession-resilient and will most likely continue to grow at a high pace, it will remain on my watch list for now.

But it seems like the market disagreed with me and although the stock was already trading for 50 times earnings, it climbed from $46 back then to almost $60 right now and an investment in the stock would have generated a return of 28% (and therefore clearly outperformed the S&P 500, which increased by only 16%).

In the following article, I will look at Rollins once again and try to answer the question of how much of a premium a wide economic moat and stable performance might be worth and at what price I would consider buying Rollins. But as always, we start by looking at the last quarterly results.

Quarterly Results

In July 2025, Rollins reported its second quarter results and while earnings per share were in line with expectations, the company did beat revenue estimates. Year-over-year, revenue increased from $891.9 million in Q2/24 to $999.5 million in Q2/25 – resulting in 12.1% year-over-year growth. Operating income also grew 8.7% year-over-year from $182.4 million in the same quarter last year to $198.3 million in this quarter. And finally, diluted net income per share increased from $0.27 in Q2/24 to $0.29 in Q2/25 – resulting in 7.4% year-over-year.

But while earnings per share grew only in the single digits, adjusted earnings per share increased 11.1% year-over-year to $0.30 and free cash flow increased even 23.2% year-over-year to $168.0 million.

Bigger Picture

When looking at the second-quarter results we see certainly solid numbers and good growth from Rollins. But we should never make the mistake and look only at one quarter, which is just a snapshot of the business. In case of Rollins, we can look at the quarterly revenue growth rates, and this is the first hint that underlines what a great business Rollins is. Of course, the quarterly revenue growth rate is fluctuating, but compared to many other companies these fluctuations are rather small and we also see growth rates accelerating in the last few years, which is a good sign.

And when looking not only at revenue, but also at the adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow conversion, we see high levels of stability since 2000. Not only is Rollins reporting more than 20 years of consecutive growth, but also has more than 75% of recurring revenue.

In the chart we also see the last two recessions and the industrial slowdown in 2015 and the chart is demonstrating that Rollins is not really affected. During the Piper Sandler 4th Annual Growth Frontiers Conferenc e, management made the following statement:

We've seen growth through just about every economic cycle, whether it be the great financial crisis back in 2008, 2009, the industrial slowdown in 2015 or more recently with COVID, we've continued to execute and successfully grow our business, continuing to see really good demand, very resilient business, an essential service with a significant amount of pricing opportunity.

During the same conference (and other conferences and presentations), management also talked about pricing and how it will contribute to growth in the years to come. Management also pointed out Rollins was able to increase prices in almost every market environment, which is another strong hint of a very stable business. During the above-mentioned conference, management also talked about pricing in excess of CPI:

And so we're continuing to focus on getting pricing that's in excess of CPI. So if CPI regresses back to a more 2% to -- a more historical trend of 2% to 3%, we really do believe that 3% to 4% is still a realistic goal for us as we think about the future. And that's helped our growth. If you go back the last 15 or 20 years prior to 2020, what you saw was growth that was maybe a 4% to 5% sort of growth profile. But more recently, what we've seen is 7% to 8% organic growth.

And management is confident it can continue to increase prices with a similar pace in the years to come. By the way, this is a strong hint for a wide economic moat around the business: If a company is able to raise prices constantly above inflation rates and not losing customers, it is a strong hint of a business that can “lock in” customers. Additionally, Rollins is an essential business, and management also sees the threat from do-it-yourself being rather limited:

Certainly, when you look at the business and you evaluate the pricing opportunities, a couple of things come to mind. One, this is very much an essential service. It's about protecting property. It's about protecting health and our consumers value it. We don't see a large amount of do-it-yourself for efforts in this space. People want to pay somebody to take care of this -- the pest and provide an environment that's conducive with their lifestyle. And so when you look at it, it's very essential. It's also in a really small ticket item, purchase item for our consumer. An annual pest control contract might be $500 or $600. It might range higher than that depending on the location you're in, and the pest you're treating, but generally, $500, $600. So when we look at it, small purchase item for our consumers, highly valued purchase item, very resilient. It's not something you can do once and forget about it. You've got to repeatedly treat the issue. And so when you look at this, what we've evaluated is the pricing opportunity and elasticity and the pricing, evaluating it through how our consumers value the service.

With a contract being worth about $500 annually, this results in about 0.1% of a typical property value (and in some regions and especially for high-end property even less). Rollins is therefore offering an essential service at a relatively low price with the potential damage risk is immense (in case of termites for example). Businesses offering services which only account for a fraction of overall costs and especially with the potential damage being immense are usually in a good position. During the JPMorgan U.S. All Stars 2025 Conference, management also underlined its strong brand names and customer trust:

So that presents us a pretty strong opportunity on the commercial side. And we have the power of the Orkin brand behind it. And what that means after being in business, Orkin next year will be -- have been in business for 125 years. And there's a lot of trust from consumers, both on the residential side and the commercial side that has been a lot of brand equity that has occurred over the last 125 years. And we feel like that puts us in a really strong position to continue to compete.

Combining all these points, we can certainly conclude that Rollins is a great business and most likely has a wide economic moat around its business. This is also visible in the chart, as Rollins has increased by more than 8,000% since the early 1990s while the S&P 500 increased about 1,800% in the same timeframe. And while Rollins actually lagged in the last 5 years, it still outperformed in the last 10 years.

In my last article I already pointed out the high return on invested capital Rollins reported in the last ten years. Only in one single year was the RoIC clearly below 20% and on average, the metric was 25.79% in the last ten years.

And Morgan Stanley mapped out the average RoIC of U.S. companies in the last six decades and with an RoIC close to 25%, Rollins is one of the best companies out there.

Continued Growth

And considering the high quality of the business as well as the wide economic moat, we can also assume that Rollins will continue to grow at a relatively high pace in the years to come. Analysts are estimating earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 9.94% in the next five years until fiscal 2029.

Looking at past growth rates, we can also see these numbers as reasonable. In the last ten years, revenue increased with a CAGR of 9.15% and earnings per share increased with a CAGR of 13.17%. Although the last ten years might not necessarily be representative for the business as we saw above average performance, revenue still increased with a CAGR of 7.14% since 2000 and adjusted EBITDA even increased by 14% annually in the same timeframe.

The company’s own growth algorithm is also expecting solid growth rates for the years to come. Revenue is expected to grow above-market due to solid organic growth as well as mergers and acquisitions. During the Piper conference, management made the following statement:

When we think about this business, we're a growth company. So what we like to provide our investors with is that steady double-digit rate of growth, which is 2/3 or 3/4 organic and 1/3 to 1/4 M&A, see tremendous opportunities to continue to provide that M&A growth and then turn that growth into double-digit earnings growth.

During the JPMorgan Conference, Rollins management also pointed out the positive impact that AI might have on the business. On the one side, its core business is threatened only very little by AI as pest control and similar services can’t be replaced by AI. On the other side, Rollins can use AI to make its business more effective – for example by making routing and scheduling more effective for the business and save costs.

Combining all the different contributors to bottom-line growth, we can be optimistic and assume growth rates in the high single digits or maybe even low double digits for the years to come.

Price Justified?

So far, we established that Rollins is a great business. Not only does the company have a wide economic moat around its business due to offering some kind of “mission-critical” service. And due to the wide economic moat, the company can continue to increase prices constantly and in almost every year above inflation.

However, Rollins is still trading for very high valuation multiples. When my last article was published in January 2025, Rollins was trading for 42 times free cash flow and 48 times earnings. Now, the stock is even trading for 45 times free cash flow and for 58 times earnings.

These are clearly high valuation multiples Rollins is trading for. However, when looking at the 10-year average P/E and P/FCF ratio we see the stock being in line with its 10-year average and suggest that Rollins might be fairly valued right now.

Additionally, let’s use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock. In a first calculation, let’s calculate with a discount rate of 10% (as this is the annual return I like to achieve, and I always use a 10% discount rate to make companies comparable – even when these companies are operating in different industries). Additionally, we calculate with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (being 484.7 million in Q2/25) and the free cash flow of the last four quarters as basis in our calculation.

When calculating with these assumptions, Rollins must grow its free cash flow between 14% and 15% annually for the next ten years followed by 6% growth till perpetuity in order to be fairly valued. I would still argue that 15% growth for the next ten years is too optimistic an assumptions and not reasonable. Due to the wide economic moat, I would argue that 6% growth till perpetuity seems achievable for the business, but we should point out that this is clearly above the recommended terminal growth rates ( according to CFI).

I always use a 10% discount rate to make different companies comparable for me. However, we can argue that different businesses should use a different WACC due to different risk profiles. In case of Rollins, we get a WACC of only 7%. As always, we are using the reported numbers for Beta, debt, cost of debt, equity and the current 10-year Treasury yield for the risk-free rate. The market risk premium I used is 6% (in line with typical assumptions).

And when calculating with a WACC of only 7%, Rollins must grow its free cash flow by only 8% for the next ten years followed by 4% growth till perpetuity to be fairly valued at this point. Without much doubt, these are growth rates Rollins can certainly achieve, and we could argue that Rollins is either fairly valued or even undervalued. Although we can argue for a lower WACC and Rollins maybe being undervalued, I still rather use my fixed discount rate of 10% to determine whether I would invest in a stock or not.

Conclusion

We could make the argument that Rollins might be fairly valued (as the current valuation multiples are in line with the 10-year average or a calculated WACC of 7% is making the stock almost a bargain) and the business is certainly a high-quality company, but I remain cautious about the stock and rate it as a “Hold”.

However, when looking at the last two decades, the chances to get Rollins really cheap might be limited as there were not steep sell-offs – not even during recessions. Considering that Rollins is performing well during recessions, this makes sense. Nevertheless, I still would not buy Rollins at this point.

In about two weeks, Rollins will report third quarter results, and we should expect Rollins to perform similar to the last few quarters and years. Revenue as well as earnings per share will continue to grow with a stable pace. That is actually one of the strengths of Rollins – the high level of predictability of the business. In the end, I don’t think third quarter results will have a huge impact on the business and assessment of the company. Of course, if the stock will move sharply in one way or the other, this will impact the assessment whether the stock is over- or undervalued.

data.title: Tilray: Another Blah Quarter
data.description:
Tilray Brands, Inc. (NASDAQ:TLRY) is jumping on some deemed positives following quarterly results, though the Canadian cannabis company still isn't producing meaningful growth. The company has long acquired assets and shifted away from cannabis, but the market too sees Tilray as a big cannabis play. My investment thesis is ultra Bearish on the stock after the recent big rally due to rescheduling hopes in the U.S., while the business isn't even focused on U.S. cannabis sales.

Blah Quarter

Tilray reported sales grew 5% in FQ1 2026 to reach $209.5 million, beating analyst estimates as follows:

While the numbers were higher compared to last FQ1, Tilray reported the lowest quarterly revenue since last year. The company reported sales of $224.5 million in the prior quarter, down 2.3% YoY despite acquiring several beverage businesses in the last year.

More importantly, the company reiterated the FY26 adjusted EBITDA outlook of $62 to $72 million. Management didn't hike the basic profit guidance, suggesting the revenue improvement and analyst beat was more a matter of analyst numbers actually matching expectations versus Tilray beating internal forecasts.

The company acquired the craft beer brands from Molson Coors (TAP) last September, so those results weren't included in the prior year's numbers. Tilray only reported revenues $10 million above the FQ1 2025 numbers despite adding Hop Valley Brewing, Terrapin Beer Co., Revolver Brewing, and Atwater Brewery from Molson Coors.

Beverage revenues actually slipped YoY to only $55.7 million despite the additional craft beer brands purchased, again a sign of chasing business versus building brands. Tilray reported the $9.4 million revenue boost due primarily to the $5.9 million increase in the Distribution business, where gross margins are only 11% now.

The other part of the revenue increase was the $4.7 million currency boost. Tilray only reported constant currency sales were up $4.7 million to $204.8 million, so half of the gains were just currency related.

Even crazier, Tilray is rallying due to the signals from President Trump on U.S. cannabis rescheduling, while the company really isn't focused on cannabis anymore. The cannabis business was only $64.5 million in the quarter and hasn't grown for years despite international operations reaching sales of $13.4 million in the August quarter.

Tilray reported an adjusted EBITDA of only $10.2 million, so less than a 5% margin. The company doesn't have a lot of positive margin and could easily see numbers dip back negative during a global recession.

Big Valuation

The stock has soared over 25% to top $2, pushing the market cap up to $2.5 billion now. Tilray trades at over 36x adjusted EBITDA targets for FY 2026.

The Canadian cannabis company only has annual cannabis revenues in the $260 million range. The stock move from below $1 seems misplaced, with the majority of the business not even being related to cannabis and definitely not focused on the U.S. market.

Tilray would have to buy into the U.S. cannabis markets to participate in any improved regulations. Those stock valuations are soaring just as fast, if not faster than Tilray, so buying assets will quickly become too expensive for a logical purchase.

Also, the market needs to remember the Distribution business is a large portion of sales while not providing any financial benefit. The division only has 11% gross margins, limiting the actual profits of the overall business.

If anything, Tilray should be valued based off the remaining business with ~$135 million in quarterly revenues and gross margins only in the mid-30% range. In fact, the adjusted gross profit was down $2.4 million YoY to only $57.5 million in FQ1 2026.

Tilray ended the quarter with a slight net debt of $4 million. The company has improved the balance sheet over the last few years, but the operating cash flow is still slightly negative, and the business spends upwards of $10 million on quarterly capex, leading to ongoing cash burn.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that stock is up on the quarterly results, but the numbers weren't very impressive. In addition, Tilray has already seen a massive rally on flimsy hopes President Trump pushes for cannabis rescheduling or anything that helps U.S. cannabis regulations. Oddly though, Tilray doesn't actually benefit from regulations providing access to the U.S. banking system or being listed on major exchanges, already being listed on the Nasdaq.

Investors should quickly sell this rally in Tilray not accompanied by improving financials or expectations.

data.title: LG Display: Positive On Client's New Products And Potential Share Gains
data.description:
I'm a bull on LG Display Co., Ltd. (NYSE:LPL) (034220.KS). The group's competitors are facing challenges. Its top customer is broadening the range of products.

The prior July 24, 2025 write-up drew attention to LPL's above-consensus 2Q25 showing.

Company Should Benefit From Client's New Offerings

Apple (AAPL) is strengthening its line-up, which is good news for LPL.

In its 20-F, LG revealed that key "end-brand customers" utilizing "our panels in their IT products" represented "42.5%, 36.8% and 35.4% of our total revenue in 2022, 2023 and 2024, respectively."

"Apple accounts for 36% of LG Display's" turnover, according to Bloomberg's early-2023 article.

It's reasonable for me to infer that this smartphone maker is LPL's biggest sales contributor.

Last month, AAPL added a number of smartwatches like "Series 11, SE3 and Ultra 3" to its portfolio.

"Series 11" wasn't a surprise, given it's the standard model that gets refreshed on a yearly basis. But "SE2" and "Ultra 2" were last released in '22 and '23, respectively.

I think LPL will perform well in FY25 on the back of a stronger-than-anticipated wearable product cycle.

Separately, SA News reported in mid-Sep that "Apple" and "its manufacturing partners could begin mass production of the foldable iPhone in India sometime in 2026." It is also mentioned that the "foldable iPhone is expected to have ultra-thin glass" whose "costs" are "between two and four times higher than conventional glass."

In my opinion, foldable devices will require more technologically-advanced components, including display panels, than their conventional counterparts. This should translate into an increase in LPL's "Average Selling Prices" or "ASPs" next year.

Also, the latest watches rolled out by AAPL mentioned above come with "Organic Light-Emitting Diode" or "OLED" screens. The same is expected for the "foldable iPhone" as well.

My late-July update emphasized that LPL's "strategic shift from LCD to higher-priced OLED products is driving ASP expansion and margin recovery."

The firm's target is raise its OLED-related top-line mix from the mid-50 percent as of end-June '25 to more than 60% going forward. This is realistic, with its major customer's new wearables and phones in mind.

LPL Is Well-Placed To Grab A Bigger Slice Of The Pie

My take is that recent developments involving LG's rivals are favorable.

"The Korea Economic Daily's (KED's)" August media report highlighted "a possible 15-year US import ban on organic light-emitting diode panels from China's BOE Technology Group Co (000725.SZ) after a preliminary ruling by the US International Trade Commission" or "USITC".

AAPL's main OLED panel suppliers are BOE, LPL, and Samsung Electronics' (OTCPK:SSNLF) (005930.KS) subsidiary "Samsung Display".

This case ultimate ruling is anticipated to be disclosed next month. The KED piece predicted that USITC will "uphold the ruling, noting its finding that BOE infringed Samsung Display’s trade secrets, as well as ongoing US-China trade tensions."

I believe that AAPL is likely to source a greater proportion of panels from Korean players like SSNLF and LPL in the future. The former shouldn't want to put its revenues at risk by being overly reliant on BOE, which is currently embroiled in a legal battle.

Earlier in July, a Nikkei Asia commentary noted that "Japan Display (OTCPK:JPDYY) (OTCPK:JNNDF) (6740.T) plans to sell its LCD and OLED panel production equipment at its Mobara fab in Chiba prefecture." This implies JPDYY will "stop making panels for the Apple Watch."

JNNDF and LPL were the only two screen suppliers for AAPL's watches. In the near-term, the latter could possibly account for 100% of the "Apple Watch's" displays until an alternative provider is identified subsequently.

The sell-side is projecting that LG's EBIT will expand by a +40% CAGR from FY25's ₩748B to FY27's ₩1.5T as per S&P Capital IQ data. Analysts have assumed a 280bps (basis points) operating margin improvement and a return to positive topline growth in the same period. I view this as achievable, considering Apple's product pipeline and competitors' woes.

Worst-Case Scenarios You Should Think About

It is possible that AAPL delays the roll-out of the foldable iPhone, and its smartwatches aren't well received.

If BOE gets a favorable judgment from USITC in November and Apple finds a new watch supplier quickly, LPL's market share might remain stagnant.

The Final Word

I continue to award a Buy rating to LG.

LPL is now trading at a depressed next twelve months' "Price-to-Sales" or "P/S" of 0.29 times (source: S&P Capital IQ). The median and mean forward P/S multiples for peers JNNDF/BOE/SSNLF are 0.70x and 0.95x, respectively.

LG's potential upside is already a substantial +62% (0.47/0.29), if it can close the valuation gap with Japan Display which boasts a 0.47x P/S.

The enterprise's expected operating profit expansion, driven by share gains and customers' new offerings, should be a key re-rating catalyst.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: From Cereal To Software: Why Next Week's Investor Days Matter
data.description:
Earnings season begins in earnest on Tuesday next week when major Financials sector companies post profit numbers. That might take the spotlight off a string of recent AI-related investments and corporate dealmaking (stress: might). With the official economic data spigot closed, happenings at the company-specific level are front and center. It’s clear that the tech boom is currently driving U.S. economic growth, but the consumer soon takes center stage as the holiday shopping season comes closer into view.

According to Adobe Analytics, online shopping volume is expected to rise by 5.3% year-on-year over the November-December stretch.1 That would be a deceleration from a stout 8.7% pace in 2024 but higher than the previous two years. We’ve mentioned the “have and have-not" economy before, and investors may get clues on the lower-end cohorts from two mainstay U.S. consumer firms in the days ahead.

The silver lining, if you can call it that, is that the broader economy seems less dependent than ever on the lower-end consumer. So far this year, GDP growth has been fueled by affluent households and a wave of AI-driven capital investment. Here are three events to keep on your radar.

General Mills Investor Day

General Mills (GIS) hosts its 2025 Investor Day the same day that JPMorgan Chase (JPM) kicks off the Q3 reporting season, October 14. Shareholders and analysts (and really anybody) can tune into the webcast to hear the latest trends in the Packaged Foods and Meats industry. Based on price action in the past year, General Mills might soon be drop-ranked to lieutenant - the stock is down 30% over the past 12 months. Shifting consumer preferences and cash-strapped lower-income households are clear and ongoing risks.

The trend toward cheaper private-label brands has hurt, but GIS did beat on earnings when it reported fiscal Q1 numbers on September 17. The $27 billion market cap Consumer Staples company also maintained its $0.61 quarterly dividend (the yield is now among the highest in the S&P 500 at 4.9% on a forward basis).2

Sentiment is low as we approach the Investor Day. When the event begins on Tuesday morning, investors should be on the lookout for details on General Mills’ five-year Accelerate strategy that was detailed at this past February’s Consumer Analyst Group of New York (CAGNY) 2025 Conference.3

It's apparent that brand-building is a key theme, but results have yet to materialize, at least according to the market. On the Q1 call, analysts questioned whether ongoing volume declines stem from structural market issues or General Mills’ botched pricing efforts. We may get color on that front next week.

The Investor Day is particularly important, as it’s possibly the last we’ll hear from GIS executives until its unconfirmed Q2 2026 earnings report on December 17.

Dollar Tree Investor Conference

Sticking with the consumer, Dollar Tree (DLTR) welcomes the Street on Wednesday, October 15. There’s also a pending speaking engagement at the NASDAQ MarketSite from Mike Creedon, CEO, and Stewart Glendinning, CFO. So, the highly volatile stock (now down almost 20% from its August high) could be on the move next week.

Dollar Tree, or “Dollar-Twenty-Five Tree” if you frequent the stores, features a high 8.4% short interest. Last month, its management team outlined a 4% to 6% comp store sales growth target as it expands its product selection.4 After a few years of tough inflation and rising labor costs, the Consumer Staples firm focuses on the expense side of the ledger, perhaps nearly as much as it does on growing revenue.

Stakeholders can expect to learn the fine points of a refreshed long-term growth strategy as well as a renewed financial outlook now that the Family Dollar divestiture is settled.5 Despite the earnings beat reported in early September, the bears have a grip on the price trend.

Lower-end consumers could be gravitating toward Walmart (WMT), the low-cost behemoth attracting a range of households across income levels. Market share trends will likely be a topic at next week’s events. Dollar Tree’s next earnings report is unconfirmed to be released on Wednesday, December 3.

Salesforce Gen AI Summit & Investor Day

Deals are being agreed to on a seemingly daily basis in the tech world right now. The AI arms race feels like it has been ratcheted up since Labor Day, but naturally, not every player has been a winner. Just this week, Salesforce (CRM) dethroned UnitedHealth Group (UNH) to claim a rather dubious honor: CRM is now the worst-performing stock on the Dow Jones Industrial Average. With shares down 29% so far in 2025, the Application Software industry company is among the most high-profile AI laggards.

CRM runs Agentforce (you may have heard about it from Matthew McConaughey), an autonomous corporate AI tool that learns, adapts, and streamlines workflows. Key to Salesforce’s success, the industry still questions the software. On Wednesday, October 15, CEO Marc Benioff, CFO Amy Weaver, and other executives will present on strategic priorities, AI innovations, and CRM’s long-term financial outlook.

The event, in conjunction with Dreamforce (the world's largest enterprise tech conference), comes after a solid Salesforce double-beat on revenue and earnings. Despite a guidance raise on September 3, the share price fell 4.9% - a third straight post-earnings slump. As far as the stock is concerned, things are not alright, alright, alright.

Benioff and the team may be on their back foot ahead of what’s a busy corporate event calendar period. Its next earnings release is unconfirmed for Tuesday, December 2.

The Bottom Line

General Mills, Dollar Tree, and Salesforce host their respective Investor Days on October 14-15 as bank earnings come in fast and AI deal flow remains high. These events, along with incoming profit reports, could offer key tells on the health of the consumer and where large enterprises are allocating their capital. We are a bit of a wet blanket this week (calling out a trio of sputtering stocks), but now is a chance for GIS, DLTR, and CRM to right the ship.

1 Online holiday spending growth set to slow to 5.3% as shoppers seek discounts, CNBC, Melissa Repko, October 6, 2025
2 General Mills Reports Fiscal 2026 First-quarter Results and Reaffirms Full-year Outlook, General Mills, September 17, 2025
3 General Mills Highlights Five Years of Progress Against Accelerate Strategy, General Mills, February 18, 2025
4 Dollar Tree, Inc. Reports Results for the Second Quarter Fiscal 2025, Dollar Tree, September 3, 2025
5 Dollar Tree Completes Sale of Family Dollar Business to Brigade Capital Management and Macellum Capital Management, Dollar Tree, July 7, 2025

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: Your Chance To Buy A Bargain 6% Yield
data.description:
Article Thesis

Bristol-Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY) is a high-quality company that oftentimes experiences large swings in its share price. Right now, its share price is at a low point, which results in a very low valuation and a highly attractive dividend yield of 6%. Its business is not cyclical, and the company has growth potential in different areas, which is why I am a fan of the stock at the current bargain valuation.

Past Coverage

I have written about Bristol-Myers Squibb here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in June 2025, around four months ago. Since then, shares are down slightly on a net basis, although they had risen above $50 for a bit after my most recent article had been published. With more than a quarter having passed since my last update on the company, and with the company reporting its earnings results since then, while we also got some news on the politics side, it is time to update my views on Bristol-Myers Squibb.

Big Pharma And Politics

High healthcare costs are a problem for many countries, including the United States. But while some believe that high healthcare costs must be caused by high drug prices primarily, that isn't true -- according to this data, drug spending is just 9% of overall healthcare spending, with way more money going to hospitals, clinics, etc. So even if drug costs could somehow be cut in half, overall healthcare spending would decline by just a couple of percentage points. I thus believe that there are more relevant tasks if politicians want to tackle high healthcare costs instead of focusing on drug costs too much.

While the Trump administration has announced tariffs on some drug imports, that is more of a problem for non-US companies. Bristol-Myers Squibb, as a US-based company, will likely be less affected. Also, since drugs have very high gross margins, tariffs would not really affect the bottom line overly much, relative to low-margin businesses like steel, automobiles, and so on, where the tariff impact is way larger.

Even if politicians want to focus on bringing down drug prices despite them making up just a minor portion of overall healthcare spending, that does not have to be a major problem for big pharma companies like BMY. Cutting out middlemen like pharmacy benefit managers is a suitable solution, and one that doesn't hurt the producer of the drugs, like BMY. A recent deal between the Trump administration and Pfizer (PFE) was very well-received by the market, showing that efforts to bring down drug prices can actually be good for pharma investors if politicians and drug companies focus on reducing bureaucratic costs, middleman costs, and so on. I would not be surprised to see similar deals with other major pharma companies in the coming weeks, possibly including Bristol-Myers Squibb.

While a regulated industry like pharma always has some political risks, I don't think political risks are overly high when it comes to BMY: Drug prices are not actually all that impactful when it comes to healthcare spending, BMY is US-based, reducing the tariff risk, and direct-to-consumer deals with the US administration seem to be good for big pharma companies.

Bristol-Myers Squibb: Avenues For Growth

Big pharma companies oftentimes do not grow at a very fast pace, as they constantly have to battle with losses of exclusivity for some of their older drugs. New drugs that are brought to the market thus have to offset declines in other drugs, where patent protection has expired. Still, Bristol-Myers Squibb should be able to deliver some growth in the coming years, I believe.

The company's most recent quarter was substantially better than what the analyst community had expected, as Wall Street analysts were forecasting a mid-single digit revenue decline while BMY actually saw a positive revenue growth rate. This was achieved despite declines in some of BMY's major legacy drugs, such as Revlimid, which BMY got via the Celgene takeover a couple of years ago, and which experienced a sales decline of close to 40%. Pomalyst, another legacy Celgene drug, saw a sales decline of more than one-quarter as well.

The good news is that as these drugs' sales are declining in size, their future declines will have a smaller impact on a company-wide basis: If, for example, a drug drops from $500 million to $250 million, the company-wide sales hit is smaller than when it drops from $1 billion to $500 million, despite the relative decline rate being the same. BMY being able to grow its sales despite the declines in some of its legacy brands was due to nice gains in assets such as Yervoy and Opdivo, which grew by 16% and 7%, respectively, versus one year earlier, with combined sales of more than $3 billion during a single quarter. Hematology drug Breyanzi did very well, seeing sales rise by a hefty 125%, now with a run rate of well north of $1 billion a year. Camzyos was another fast-growing drug during Q2, with sales jumping 87% to just above a quarter-billion dollars during the quarter. The top-line contribution for BMY of these fast-growing drugs will rise quickly if they maintain their very positive momentum going forward.

Bristol-Myers Squibb expects many major data points in the foreseeable future, including on BMY's Alzheimer's Disease candidate and on Krazati in first-line non-small cell lung cancer, which are two very big markets, with Iza-bren in solid tumors being interesting as well, I believe. Recent deals with companies such as BioNTech (BNTX) have further strengthened BMY's pipeline, and the company has the financial firepower to engage in M&A in the future, too:

While BMY has a considerable debt position, its business is throwing off a lot of cash -- around $15 billion over the last year, with capital expenditures already being accounted for. The dividend costs around $5 billion per year, thus the company has $10 billion in surplus cash that is available for M&A, debt reduction, or share repurchases. All of these would create value in the long run:

- M&A would strengthen the pipeline and add future revenue and profit

- Debt reduction would shift value from debt holders to equity holders, assuming BMY's enterprise value remains unchanged, while also reducing interest expenses and thereby growing profits

- Share repurchases give all remaining investors a larger portion of the pie and grow cash flow per share and earnings per share

I think pursuing a mix of these is a good idea, but due to the very low valuation BMY trades at today -- its earnings yield is around 15% -- share repurchases seem highly attractive to me right now.

With tailwinds from an aging population in many major markets that requires more healthcare, which should be good for all pharma companies, BMY thus has a positive growth outlook, I believe: It owns fast-growing new drugs, its cash cows like Opdivo keep growing as well, it has a deep pipeline, keeps forging deals with companies like BNTX, and buybacks could add to its earnings per share growth on top of that.

BMY: A Nice Yield At A Low Price

Today, at just below $45 per share, BMY trades at a little less than 7x net earnings. For a company with a resilient business model and a not-great-but-solid growth outlook, that doesn't seem fair, I believe. When we look at BMY's enterprise value to EBITDA multiple, which also accounts for debt and cash on the balance sheet, we see a very low valuation as well:

In recent months, BMY's valuation has declined meaningfully, making it an even better value today. At the same time, the price decline has made BMY's dividend yield climb to 6%, which I deem very attractive, especially when combined with a payout ratio of less than 40% -- making the dividend pretty safe.

All in all, Bristol-Myers Squibb looks quite attractive here, I believe. While politics are a factor to consider, I don't think the risks are very high, and the advantages, like a great yield and a bargain valuation, easily outweigh the risks.

data.title: Silicom Struggles To Make Ends Meet But Trades For Liquidation Value
data.description:
Silicom (NASDAQ:SILC) is an Israeli developer of network hardware, traditionally selling to on-premise server OEMs, which is slowly turning into more cloud-oriented products.

The company suffered a double-whammy of a destocking cycle that started in 2022, coupled with a missed shift in the technological landscape. This led to a collapse in sales to almost 1/3 of their peak ($58 million in FY24 vs $150 million in FY22). At this scale, the company is unprofitable.

Seen from this perspective, a hardware company that has been left somewhat behind in technology and that cannot cover its expenses should not be worth much. Indeed, that is the case, with the company trading for basically a small premium over its cash holdings. This provides some degree of optionality, which, without making it suitable for long-term investing, makes it interesting for speculation. I believe the name is a Hold, but consider that it could be a speculative Buy for some aggressive readers.

Network adapter equipment

Silicom's business has been the design of modular add-ons to servers, providing additional functionalities, like encryption, acceleration, data compression, redirection, or switching.

In particular, the company specializes in selling a variety of modules, each with different configurations. This allows customers with specific needs to select a customized application.

The company sells mostly via integration with server OEMs. These OEMs cannot (or could not) get all of the customization needed from the original chip or PCB designers (Intel, Broadcom, NVIDIA), and therefore used Silicom's products to enhance the functionality of their products and serve more niche markets.

R&D with a focus on D

From the financials, product, and even name, Silicom is a tech, R&D-focused company. Almost half of its employees work in R&D, and if we remove manufacturing employees, research would be 75% of the company (20-F under Total Employees). R&D also makes up 2/3 of the company's OpEx ($20 million out of $30 million in FY24), and has not been cut even under the company's current challenging situation

However, three factors lead me to add some nuance around the technological focus of the company.

First, the company never achieved super exciting gross margins (peaking at 40% over a decade ago). High gross margins, even in hardware businesses, are a sign that the product can be sold for a premium, which embeds the 'intangible technological component'. Indeed, this is the reason why software, which is almost entirely intangible, has 80/90% gross margins sometimes.

Second, the company records several patents between 2012 and 2015, then one in 2018, and then no other (see 20-F under Intellectual Property). This fall in patenting activity is coincident with a fall in gross margins that continues up to this point.

And finally, the company seems to have lost track of the technological trend, or has been unable to steer the course of its developments, as seen in its collapsing revenues (more on this below).

This leads me to believe that at least part of that R&D expense is what can be considered 'Design'. That is, work performed specifically for a customer in order to win a design (get a component inside of the customer's product), which does not confer a specifically valuable technological advantage that can be patented or a moat built around.

This type of Design-Win dynamic is very common in all kinds of industries where a group of component designers and manufacturers work around a central OEM. One such example is vehicle manufacturing. These companies can look innovative because they have lots of engineers, but are not necessarily developing any cutting-edge products.

Design-to-win companies tend to suffer from not incredible economics and low bargaining power, given that the OEM controls distribution and has access to clients, and that it can purchase from a series of component designers. The company invests in designing a particular component before landing a design-win, and therefore has an incentive to recover those investments by winning the design, even if the margins or conditions are not the best.

A collapse in sales that cannot be blamed on inventories

The company had already shown some challenges in decreasing margins, but had not posted problems with revenues. Quite the contrary, even if growth was not fast, the company was moving upwards in revenues.

This changed after 2022, since when the company's sales have fallen to almost 1/3 of their peak, in less than 3 years.

On the MD&A of its FY24 20-F (which includes explanations for FY23), the company reports that the fall in revenues was generated mostly by a destocking cycle at its customers' level. This can be understandable, given that many industries suffered from overstocking and then destocking in the 2021/22 aftermath period.

However, it cannot cover the entirety of the process, first because of the degree of the decline in sales, and second because that kind of cycle tends to repeat over time, but we can't find any such instances of such a magnitude in the company's revenues in the past.

There are two indications that at least one additional driver is technological. First, the company recognizes on its 20-F (under Trends) that the movement form on-promise servers to cloud-based servers has made some of its product lines obsolete, while enhancing others. Second, the company announced in early 2024 a 5-year plan to recover its profitability, which included phasing off some product lines (for example, Server Adapters) and focusing on others (like Smart Platforms), which are more in line with new technological trends.

Unprofitable at its current scale

After the revenues collapsed, the company cut some expenses, with the number of employees falling from 306 in 2022 to 229 in 2024 (not reported for 2Q25 so far). The enormous majority of these cuts were in Manufacturing, which makes sense if the company is selling less.

However, the company is currently not able to cover most of its operating expenses at this scale, and has maintained a loss of around $13/15 million operationally per year, which is not improving in recent quarters.

In recent earnings calls, the company has not talked about continuing with OpEx cuts. Rather, I believe it expects to grow into profitability (more on this in the valuation section).

Financially sound

If there is one strength that Silicom has, it is its balance sheet. The company has no debt, has a positive working capital of almost $35 million, and has cash and securities of $79 million (2Q25 results, $52 million cash and equivalents, $12 million current marketable securities, and $15 million non-current marketable securities).

This allows the company to sustain the above losses for years without entering into any potential financial problems. It also allows SILC to carry out some share buybacks, specifically almost $10 million in each FY23 and FY24 (see historical cashflows).

Unfortunately, the company does not publish cash flows on a quarterly basis. However, looking at the FY24 cash flows, they were very moderately negative on an operating basis without working capital. The company posted a net loss of almost $14 million, but when we add back depreciation (~$2 million), SBC (~$3 million), and non-cash impairments (~$6 million), the cash outflow from operations is actually closer to $3 million only. In addition, the company reduced its working capital, generating a positive $18 million inflow from operations.

If we look at this year, I would expect the cash flow losses to be larger. The reason is that this year, the company has been posting similar operating losses, but in this case, they cannot be blamed on non-cash impairments. Still, considering similar D&A and SBC levels to last year, the cash losses should not be above $8 million before working capital, indicating the company has years at this level of operations before any financial trouble appears.

Not interesting for investment, potentially for speculation

What would the company need in order to become profitable, assuming that it does not cut (nor grow) its OpEx?

From OpEx of $30 million, and assuming a current gross margin of about 32%, SILC would need about $94 million in revenues to break even operationally. This is almost 60% and $36 million above its current TTM revenue levels.

On its 5-year plan, the company set an objective of $3 in EPS by 2028.

How much that level of EPS represents in net income is hard to say without knowing if the company has continued to repurchase shares, or if it will do so even more in the future. Based on the 2Q25 average weighted shares (5.7 million), it would mean about $17 million in net income, or close to $175 million in revenues, assuming no increase in OpEx or improvement in gross margins ($17 million in net income is $22 million in pre-tax income before 25% taxes, which added to $30 million in OpEx and 32% gross margins implies $175 million in revenues).

SILC is starting to show some signs of improvement, with 7 to 9 design wins guided for FY25 (about 4 already announced, each for between $1 and $3 million annual run rate at full ramp up). Based on a $2 million average win, and assuming no design losses (super optimistic assumption in such a technological market), the company could add $18 million in revenues per year at this rate, or reach break-even in 2027, and probably not meet the $3 EPS objective by 2028. That is with two optimistic expectations, like flat OpEx, and no loss of previous contracts.

However, that scenario is heavily discounted in today's stock price. Indeed, the company basically trades for its liquidation value (net working capital plus cash and securities of about $113 million versus a market cap of about $100 million).

That means the market already discounts the current scenario, where SILC generates no profitability, and eventually either liquidates or eats its cash balances slowly. The positive scenarios become optionality.

In my opinion, given the company's challenging competitive position (explained in the Design section), the failures of the past in maintaining market, and the fact that it requires significant growth to reach break-even, all lead me to believe the company will not generate profits soon, or ever, and that it is therefore not attractive for investment (as it is not planning to liquidate).

For this reason, I consider the name a Hold. However, I do believe that some readers might find the company attractive for speculative purposes, given that the most probable negative scenario is discounted, whereas the much less probable but still existing scenarios of success are not.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Air Liquide: A Hidden Backbone Of The AI Era And The Hydrogen Economy
data.description:
Air Liquide (OTCPK:AIQUF, OTCPK:AIQUY) is one of the world’s leading industrial gas and specialty chemical companies potentially positioned well to benefit from industry tailwinds for clean-energy hydrogen projections, electrification and semiconductor demand. As part of my ongoing expansion into the chemical space, I am initiating coverage for Air Liquide to assess whether its leadership in gasses and growth drivers in tech and energy transition are strong enough to offset a soft macroeconomic backdrop.

Air Liquide Specializes In Industrial Gasses With Energy Transition Growth Opportunities

The core business of Air Liquide is Gas & Services which generates 97% of the revenues, the remainder is Engineering & Technologies. The company has five business segments:

  1. Large Industries
  2. Industrial Merchant
  3. Healthcare
  4. Electronics
  5. Engineering & Technologies

The Large Industries segment provides large volumes of oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen and other industrial gasses to heavy industry customers such as steelmakers, petrochemical plants and energy companies. These sales are typically generated through long-term contracts providing some shielding. However, the segment is not immune to the cyclical nature of the end markets. Growth drivers include growing demand for hydrogen for refining and clean fuel and decarbonization.

The Industrial Merchant sector focuses on providing smaller customers in the food and beverage, electronics and pharmaceutical industries with gasses. The company benefits from growth in pharmaceutical and electronics demand. This segment benefits from strong pricing power.

The Healthcare segment provides medical gases to hospitals, clinics and home-care services. The demand for healthcare never stops and that makes the segment counter-cyclical. We also note that with higher life expectancy, demand for home-based care is also rising and technological innovation in healthcare, often requiring medical gasses, also never stops.

Air Liquide provides high purity gasses and advanced materials for semiconductor manufacturing and photovoltaics. The use of gasses in semiconductor manufacturing is often not highlighted, but without gasses manufacturing would not be possible. Current high demand for chips provides a solid growth driver for gasses and advanced materials.

The Engineering and Technologies segment builds and sells gas production and purification plants, hydrogen and carbon dioxide solutions.

Putting it all together, I do believe that the company has significant exposure to cyclical markets and also the semiconductor end market heavily depends on investment cycles. At the same time, the healthcare market is counter-cyclical, and I believe Air Liquide could play a pivot role in clean energy projects, particularly clean hydrogen and decarbonization projects.

Air Liquide Shows Resilience In A Turbulent Market

In the first half of the year, the company reported sales growth of 2.6% to €13.7 billion and 1.8% comparable growth. EBITDA increased 5.1% to €3.8 billion indicating margin expansion from 28.6% to 29.3%. This was driven by pricing strength and initiatives to simplify the organization. Operating income increased 5.2% and 7.2% on comparable basis to €2.74 billion indicating margin expansion from 19.4% to almost 20%. Excluding the impact of fluctuations in energy prices, which are an input cost to the business, the margins would have increased 100 bps.

Large industry sales increased 1% to €1.74 billion in Q2 on solid activity in the Americas and the start up of an Air Separation Unit in China. Industrial Merchant sales grew 2% to €3.05 billion on flat gas volumes and lower hardware sales offset by solid pricing power. Electronics sales declined 2% to €600 million but still showed growth for the first half of the year. Healthcare sales rose 5% to €1.09 billion driven by pricing and volumes for Home Healthcare solutions.

Air Liquide Deserves A Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Price targets reflect the assessment of the listing at the Euronext Paris.

Air Liquide is not a high-growth name, but I do believe that the company offers upside. I am assigning a buy rating with a $232.81 price target, indicating 18% upside. We do note the exposure to cyclical markets. However, the company is expected to grow EBITDA and free cash flow by 6-7 percent annually, driving the double-digit upside that I typically look for.

Conclusion: Air Liquide Has Appealing Exposure

The exposure to large industrial companies may not seem appealing. However, also cyclical markets rebound and I do believe that within the cyclical markets demand for clean energy such as green hydrogen will increase. Furthermore, we note the exposure to countercyclical market, pricing power and an appealing combination of service the semiconductor markets and opportunities for green hydrogen and decarbonization. As a result, I do believe the current stock price offers a compelling entry point for investors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
